title: Industry Secrets We Wish We Knew Before Graduating | Staff Eng Talk @ UCLA
guest_role: Staff Engineer at Google by 28; co-speaker with host Ryan Peterman at a UCLA UPE talk
key_takeaways: Luck influences promotions, but you can expand your 'luck surface area' by taking initiative to seek new opportunities and being prepared when they arrive.

Perfect code isn't enough: the best engineers weren't necessarily the top programmers from college but had balanced technical and soft skills — influencing others, collaborating in teams, and writing concisely.
People decide promotions, so you must advocate for yourself; visibility is an important part of growing your career alongside doing quality work.
Both speakers would choose Big Tech over startups straight out of college for instant credibility and foundational skills, then pivot to startups later with brand equity and expertise.
Lines of code != impact: the industry rewards results, not effort; writing less code while achieving greater impact is preferable.
Big Tech won't make you crazy rich: climbing levels is a reliable path to financial stability and freedom, but exceptional wealth requires breakout startup opportunities.
Job hopping helps early in a career, but promotions beyond Senior/Staff depend on influence and credibility that take time to build at each company, making late-career hopping counterproductive.
LLMs are productivity tools like the many non-AI tools engineers already use; SWE jobs look secure for roughly five years, with longer-term impacts unpredictable.

For college recruiting, resume projects are the minimum; persistence and joining smaller companies early accelerates experience. For internship return offers, finish well-defined projects quickly with high-quality code and propose improvements afterward.

notable_quotes.speaker: Ryan Peterman
notable_quotes.quote: There's so much that no one taught me in college that I had to learn in industry myself.

notable_quotes.speaker: Ryan Peterman
notable_quotes.quote: If that part isn't good, then most people won't watch the whole thing.

notable_quotes.quote: Visibility is therefore an important part of growing your career.
